sourceMovie.title: The Devil Wears Prada
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: Audiences latched onto The Devil Wears Prada (2006) as a drama theatrical experience: big enough for a group watch, specific enough to reward a second viewing once you know where it's headed. The drama and comedy blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: The Proposal
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The drama energy of The Devil Wears Prada is alive in The Proposal (2009), filtered through a different story but the same emotional register. It earns its place on this list with a funny payoff that rivals the original pick.

recommendations.title: Morning Glory
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Morning Glory (2010) operates in the same drama as The Devil Wears Prada, but with its own distinct voice and pacing. The funny payoff will feel satisfyingly familiar without retreading the same ground.

recommendations.title: Never Been Kissed
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Never Been Kissed (1999) belongs to the same conversation as The Devil Wears Prada — both anchor their drama in comedy emotion that doesn't let go. The funny undercurrent connects them more than any plot summary would suggest.

recommendations.title: A Lot Like Love
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If The Devil Wears Prada's drama stayed with you, A Lot Like Love (2005) delivers the same sensation through an entirely different story. The comedy through-line is the real common thread between them.

recommendations.title: The Hating Game
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Where The Devil Wears Prada excels in drama, The Hating Game (2021) doubles down on comedy to comparable effect. The tonal similarities run deep enough that watching both back-to-back feels intentional.

recommendations.title: Stolen Kisses
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Stolen Kisses (1968) is the natural next step after The Devil Wears Prada: same drama DNA, different perspective. The funny streak and comedy elevate it well above a simple genre exercise.

recommendations.title: Bed and Board
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Fans of The Devil Wears Prada who respond to its drama will find Bed and Board (1970) immediately familiar. Both films share a comedy sensibility without ever feeling like carbon copies of each other.

recommendations.title: My Old Lady
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: There's a reason The Devil Wears Prada fans keep landing on My Old Lady (2014) — the drama is unmistakably related even when the plots diverge. Both films operate on the same emotional frequency, especially in their third acts.

recommendations.title: Antoine and Colette
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Antoine and Colette draws from the same drama well as The Devil Wears Prada, channeling comedy in ways that feel unmistakably related. The 1962 film makes a natural companion watch — similar in spirit but distinctly its own.

recommendations.title: Back to the Future
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Back to the Future (1985) laid groundwork for the tone and themes that The Devil Wears Prada later expanded. If The Devil Wears Prada resonated for you, this predecessor shows where that emotional DNA began.

faq.question: What should I watch after The Devil Wears Prada?
faq.answer: Try The Proposal, Morning Glory, Never Been Kissed—each shares DNA with The Devil Wears Prada while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is The Devil Wears Prada?
faq.answer: The Devil Wears Prada (2006) is best described as Drama, Comedy. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is The Devil Wears Prada part of a franchise?
faq.answer: Yes—it's part of the "The Devil Wears Prada Collection" storyline. You can enjoy it standalone, but sequels and tie-ins build on characters and continuity established here.
